summary: Simon Späti documents his transition from a 5-year-old MacBook Pro M1 Max to a Lenovo ThinkBook running Arch Linux with DHH's Omarchy distribution. After 15 years on macOS, he finds the switch surprisingly smooth thanks to Omarchy's opinionated setup that mirrors keyboard-driven workflows. While acknowledging trade-offs like worse battery life, fan noise, and missing tools like Snagit, he emphasizes the joy of customization and learning. The post details his pre-switch requirements, app replacements found for most needs, and hardware choice rationale, ultimately concluding that the freedom to modify his OS outweighs the polish of macOS.
key_points: Omarchy makes Arch Linux approachable for macOS power users by providing sensible defaults for screens, media keys, hibernation, and keyboard-driven navigation out of the box
Most macOS apps have Linux alternatives (Raycast→Walker, Karabiner→Kanata, Sync→Filen), though screenshot tools like Snagit remain unmatched
The trade-offs are real: worse battery life, constant fan noise, occasional breakage requiring manual fixes, and no native apps for tools like Claude Desktop or Microsoft Teams
The customization freedom is the killer feature - being able to script shortcuts for anything, change themes system-wide, and modify the OS to personal preferences
MacBook hardware quality (5+ year lifespan, no fans, great battery) remains superior, but the frustration with Apple's walled garden and breaking tools like Yabai motivated the switch
money_quotes: If I don't like something on my OS, I just change it. Or if I want to improve a certain way of working, a quick script, a new package, and I'm just flying.
I never thought that an OS can be customizable to my liking.
The sky is the limit, literally.
It's so fun to set up your OS in a way that makes you more productive, or gives you joy just by the looks of it.
But stuff like this makes me scared that I can run a wrong command and ruin my perfect setup. I need a backup!
The quality of MacBooks is just another level. I had 3 or 4 so far since 2010, and each of them held at least 5 years. Crazy good.
Before, Windows was unusable and with Arch in the back, it runs almost like a brand-new laptop.
key_insight: The freedom to fully customize your operating system and workflow can outweigh the polish and convenience of macOS, especially for developers frustrated by Apple's increasingly restrictive approach.
